messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following energy supplier customer privacy policy for GDPR compliance:

"PowerGrid collects your name, address, meter readings, and energy consumption data to supply electricity and manage your account. We process this under our contract with you and legal obligations as a licensed energy supplier. Smart meter data is collected every 30 minutes and retained for 5 years as required by energy regulations. You may opt down to daily or monthly readings by contacting us. Bills and payment records are kept for 6 years. You have the right to access, correct, port, restrict, erase, and object to your data. Contact dataprotection@powergrid.com or our DPO at dpo@powergrid.com. We notify the ICO within 72 hours of a breach. We use Salesforce (EU instance) as our CRM provider under a signed DPA."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PASS] Contract performance (Art. 6(1)(b)) and legal obligation (Art. 6(1)(c)) both stated and appropriate for energy supply.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Smart meter data can reveal household occupancy patterns and health-related routines. This inference risk is not acknowledged.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PASS] Energy supply, account management, regulatory compliance, and billing are all stated as clear purposes.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [PASS] 30-minute smart meter intervals are standard and regulatorily required. Opt-down to daily or monthly readings shows proportionality awareness.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] Smart meter data: 5 years (regulatory). Bills and payments: 6 years (financial legal obligation). Both clearly stated and justified.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PASS] Access (Art. 15), rectification (Art. 16), erasure (Art. 17), restriction (Art. 18), portability (Art. 20), and objection (Art. 21) all listed explicitly.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [PASS] Salesforce EU instance named as CRM processor with signed DPA.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [PASS] Salesforce EU instance confirms EEA processing.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [PASS] DPO contact disclosed at dpo@powergrid.com.

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [PASS] 72-hour ICO notification stated.

VERDICT: Compliant
VIOLATIONS: None
RECOMMENDATIONS: No changes required. Consider acknowledging that smart meter data can infer household behaviour patterns and referencing the privacy impact assessment conducted for this processing.
